digest.top_line: PEACE-2: pelvic RT null in very high-risk localized prostate with PSMA staging + 3yr ADT (HR 0.81, p=0.09), challenging POP-RT in the modern systemic era.
digest.tldr: PEACE-2 null pelvic RT (HR 0.81, p=0.09) conflicts with POP-RT, raising whether PSMA PET + 3yr ADT erases nodal RT benefit in very high-risk disease. Breast dominated by volume: de-escalation signals from boost (Dutch registry, IMPORT HIGH 10yr), PBI (DBCG RT Natural), and hypofrax (DBCG HYPO). OLIGOMA, first breast-specific phase III MDT RCT, shows PFS HR 0.48 in oligoMBC.

sites.intro: Pelvic RT intensification is this meeting's central prostate question: PEACE-2 null, PACE-NODES toxicity acceptable, PIVOTALboost safe — efficacy endpoints across all three still pending.

studies.name: RADIOSA
studies.tldr: RADIOSA post-hoc: SBRT+ADT vs SBRT alone, median MFS not reached vs 16.6mo, HR 0.39 in oligorecurrent prostate.
details: 📊 Metastatic progression: 19/51 (37.3%) Arm B vs 32/51 (62.7%) Arm A; median MFS not reached vs 16.6mo (95% CI 12.83-NA)
🔍 Phase II RCT, N=102, 1:1 randomization; SBRT alone (Arm A) vs SBRT + 6mo ADT (Arm B); median f/u 49.23mo (95% CI 42.47-54.8)
📊 Eugonadal MFS (post-testosterone recovery) also favored Arm B, p<0.05; all but 2 Arm B pts recovered testosterone within f/u
⚠️ Post-hoc analysis of a phase II trial; MFS is a surrogate endpoint, not OS; N=102 limits power; no biomarker stratification
🔗 Eugonadal MFS benefit suggests durable synergy beyond castration period, extending prior RADIOSA primary endpoint findings
❓ Whether short-term ADT duration (6mo) is optimal vs longer course in oligorecurrent setting remains open

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc analysis of a phase II trial; N=102, MFS surrogate endpoint, not powered for OS. Hypothesis-generating for SBRT+ADT intensification.
verdict.audience: Oligorecurrent prostate cancer, SBRT candidates, hormone-sensitive

studies.name: PEACE-2
studies.tldr: PEACE-2: pelvic RT added to ADT + dose-escalated EBRT shows no significant cPFS benefit in very high-risk localized prostate cancer (HR 0.81, p=0.088).

details.text: 📊 Primary endpoint cPFS and key secondaries — WPRT vs prostate-only RT
🔗 Cross-trial comparison: WPRT efficacy vs prostate-only RT (HR <1 favors WPRT)

details: 🔍 Phase III, 4-arm RCT (arms A–D: prostate vs pelvic RT ± cabazitaxel x4); very high-risk localized CaP ≥2 risk factors (Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20), N0M0 on conventional or choline PET/CT; 3yr ADT; prostate dose 78 Gy EQD2; median f/u ~5.5yr (interim at ESTRO 2026)

⚠️ Key design differences vs POP-RT: longer ADT (3 vs 2yr), modern PSMA PET/CT staging, higher dose escalation, and contemporary systemic intensification — each of these may reduce residual benefit attributable to elective pelvic RT alone
⚠️ Interim analysis with ~5.5yr median f/u; OS and CSS data not mature. PSMA PET/CT staging likely excluded occult metastatic pts who might have benefited most from pelvic nodal coverage

❓ With <10% prostate cancer mortality at 10yr, does this population truly represent 'very high-risk'? Biomarker/genomic stratification needed to identify pts where pelvic RT intensification still adds value

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT directly contradicts POP-RT signal; WPRT adds no significant benefit under modern staging and dose-escalated ADT intensification.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized prostate cancer (≥2 of: Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20), N0M0, 3yr ADT planned

studies.name: PACE-NODES
studies.tldr: PACE-NODES: pelvic nodal SBRT adds short-term GI toxicity (28% vs 21% CTCAE ≥2) vs prostate-only SBRT; resolved by 12wk, no GU difference.
details: 📊 CTCAE ≥2 GI symptoms: 28% PPN-SBRT vs 21% P-SBRT over 12-week period; difference resolved with no gap at 12wk
📊 No difference in acute GU toxicity clinician- or patient-reported; EPIC-26 bowel domain diverged at 4wk only
🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1166, high-risk PCa (T3a-T4 and/or GS 8-10 and/or PSA >20 ng/ml), 12-36mo ADT; 36.25Gy/5f prostate ± 25Gy/5f pelvic nodes on alternate days
⚠️ 11% PPN-SBRT vs 4% P-SBRT did not receive allocated treatment, mostly planning constraints; primary EP (time to BCF) not yet mature, this is interim toxicity data only
🔗 POP-RT and PEACE-2 established pelvic nodal RT benefit with moderate hypofractionation; PACE-NODES extends the question to 5-fraction SBRT fractionation
❓ Primary efficacy EP (BCF-FS) pending; late GI/GU toxicity and cancer control outcomes unknown
studies.key_figure_caption: CTCAE ≥2 GI: 28% PPN-SBRT vs 21% P-SBRT over 12-week period. Resolved by 12wk. No difference in GU toxicity.

verdict.rationale: Phase 3 RCT with adequate N but primary efficacy endpoint not yet mature; presentation is interim acute toxicity data only.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate cancer (T3a-T4, GS 8-10, or PSA>20), 12-36mo ADT

studies.name: PIVOTALboost
studies.tldr: PIVOTALboost: 20-fraction focal boost + pelvic node RT safe at 2yr; late G2+ bowel/bladder not increased vs prostate-only IMRT.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1465 randomised, 39 UK centres; 20-fraction IMRT ± pelvic node RT ± focal boost; high-risk localised prostate; 1° EP biochemical/clinical failure
📊 Early bowel G2+: transiently elevated with pelvic node RT, resolved by 18 weeks; specific early-phase arm rates not quantified in source
⚠️ Safety secondary endpoints only presented; primary EP (biochemical/clinical failure) not reported at this session
🔗 CHHiP established 20-fraction prostate IMRT safety; FLAME (normofractionation focal boost) showed no toxicity increase; PIVOTALboost extends both to pelvic node RT in 20-fraction context
❓ Whether boost or pelvic node RT improves biochemical control awaits primary EP readout

verdict.rationale: Phase 3 RCT safety-only secondary EP; 2-yr G2+ rates consistent with prior hypofractionated prostate RT data. Primary efficacy EP not yet presented.

studies.name: PRIME
studies.tldr: PRIME (interim): 5 fx SBRT vs 25 fx moderate hypofrax + pelvic RT in high-risk/node+ prostate; BFFS not yet mature, G3+ toxicity <1% both arms.
details: 🔍 Phase III non-inferiority, N=434, high-risk/very high-risk or node-positive non-metastatic prostate; both arms: whole-pelvis RT + 2yr ADT; PSMA PET/CT staging allowed
💊 Arm A (SBRT): 36.25 Gy/5 fx (7.25 Gy/fx) every other day; Arm B: ~68 Gy/25 fx (2.7 Gy/fx); both arms pelvic nodes 25 Gy/5 fx
📊 1° EP (BFFS, Phoenix): no HR reported at interim; early 1-2yr data show no signal of inferiority for SBRT arm; 4-5yr mature data awaited
🔗 vs HYPO-RT-PC: node-negative, no ADT, no pelvic RT; 10yr FFS HR 0.84 (0.69-1.03), non-inferiority confirmed. PRIME extends to node-positive with mandatory ADT + whole-pelvis RT
⚠️ Interim analysis only; 1-2yr median f/u insufficient for BFFS in pts on 2yr ADT; no efficacy HR yet reported; non-inferiority formally unevaluable
❓ Will BFFS non-inferiority hold at 4-5yr? Optimal SIB dose to PET-positive pelvic nodes?
studies.key_figure_caption: Late G2+ GU p=0.59; G2+ GI p=0.20; G3+ GU/GI <1% both arms (1-2yr follow-up).

verdict.rationale: Phase III but interim only; primary BFFS endpoint requires 4-5yr follow-up; no efficacy HR reported; non-inferiority unevaluable at this stage.
verdict.audience: High-risk/node-positive non-metastatic prostate, ADT (2yr), whole-pelvis RT

open_questions: Does PSMA PET staging + 3yr ADT + dose-escalated EBRT render elective pelvic nodal RT redundant in N0 very high-risk disease, or is PEACE-2 underpowered at interim?
Will PACE-NODES primary BCF-FS readout show efficacy benefit despite the modest, transient GI toxicity seen at interim?
Can PRIME 5-fraction whole-pelvis SBRT achieve BFFS non-inferiority in node-positive disease at the 4-5yr mature readout?

sites.intro: De-escalation dominated: boost omission in low-risk pts, PBI for elderly BCS, 10-yr hypofrax durability confirmed, and locoregional omission post-NAT still gated on B-51.

studies.name: OLIGOMA
studies.tldr: OLIGOMA: MDT + systemic therapy vs systemic alone in oligometastatic breast, mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4mo, HR 0.48.
details: 📊 Co-primary QoL (EORTC QLQ-C30) at 12 wks: 72.2 vs 74.3, diff -2.1 (95% CI -9.2 to 5.1); non-inferiority margin -10 pts, met
🔍 Phase III RCT, N=87 (43 exp / 44 ctrl); oligoMBC any line, ≤5 mets; SABR/SBRT to all lesions + ongoing systemic; NCT04495309
🔍 >80% had 1-3 mets; ~2/3 lesions were bone; ~75% first-line endocrine or chemo; majority ER/PR+ HER2-neg
⚠️ Recruitment stopped early at <20% of initial and <40% of amended target N; underpowered, wide CI on HR (0.25-0.91); OS data not reported in source
🔗 Adds to SABR-COMET, ORIOLE, STOMP in MDT for oligomet disease; breast-specific first RCT showing PFS benefit
❓ Which pts benefit most unclear; comprehensive biomarker analysis pending; OS and longer f/u needed

verdict.rationale: Early stopped, N=87, wide HR CI (0.25-0.91); no OS data. First breast-specific RCT for MDT but underpowered for definitive conclusions.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic breast cancer, any line, ≤5 mets, predominantly ER/PR+ HER2-neg

studies.name: EORTC IM-MS (22922/10925)
studies.tldr: EORTC IM-MS at 20yr: OS null (HR 1.00); BCM benefit (HR 0.82) offset by non-BCM excess (HR 1.26) from late RT cardiotoxicity.
details: 🔍 pN0 subgroup: DFS 53.9% vs 53.6% HR 0.93 (p=0.318), DMFS 67.2% vs 67.4% HR 0.93 (p=0.397) — no benefit
🔗 DBCG IMN2 (Nielsen AWM, Lancet Reg Health Eur 2024): modern RT MHD 1.2/2.3 Gy right/left (4-9x lower than this trial) showed IM-MS RT improved BCM and OS in node+ at 15yr
⚠️ Era caveat: EORTC heart doses far exceed modern planning; non-BCM mortality excess likely not reproducible with current technique

verdict.rationale: Mature phase III, null OS at 20yr: BCM gain offset by non-BCM excess from legacy high heart doses; era-specific result.
verdict.audience: Stage I-III breast cancer incl. pN0, post-surgical, planning adjuvant RT

studies.name: HypoG-01
studies.tldr: HypoG-01 patterns-of-failure analysis: LRR infrequent (20/118 events), no difference between 40 Gy/15 fx and 50 Gy/25 fx arms.
details: 🔍 Pre-planned secondary analysis of phase III RCT; N=1,260, median f/u 4.8 yrs; ITT; 3-week (40 Gy/15 fx) vs 5-week (50 Gy/25 fx) + tumour-bed boost
📊 LRR site classification: 20/30 sites in-volume (67%); 19/30 nodal sites, mainly L1 & L2
📊 Patterns of failure not obviously different between 3-week and 5-week arms
🔗 Consistent with prior hypofractionation equivalence data (START-A/B); adds failure-pattern granularity not previously reported
⚠️ Secondary analysis only; no HR or p-values for arm-vs-arm LRR comparison reported in source; event numbers small (LRR n=20)
❓ Whether marginal-zone recurrences differ by arm at longer f/u remains open; nodal L1/L2 predominance may inform future CTV expansion
studies.key_figure_caption: First oncological events (N=118): iLRR 16%, IDR 51%, second malignancy 31%. LRR in-volume: 20/30 sites (67%). Nodal sites: 19/30.

verdict.rationale: Pre-planned secondary analysis of completed phase III; supports adequacy of ESTRO contouring guidelines with 40 Gy/15 fx, consistent with START equivalence.
verdict.audience: Early-stage breast cancer, post-operative whole-breast RT, 3-week vs 5-week hypofractionation

studies.name: Dutch Breast Boost Era Analysis
studies.tldr: IBTR <3% at 10yr for 0-2 risk factor pts post-BCT in modern systemic therapy era; boost may be omitted in this group.
details: 📊 Cumulative incidence IBTR by risk factor group (no boost / boost)
🔍 Retrospective Dutch registry cohort, BCT pts 2012-2016; IBTR identified via pathology report algorithm; Assisi 2024 thresholds used (<3% at 10yr with boost, <6% without boost)
📐 Only ≥3 risk factor subgroup with boost exceeded Assisi threshold at 10yr (3.3%); all other subgroups remained below thresholds

🔗 Boost benefit established by EORTC 22881/10882 (Bartelink, Lancet Oncol 2015): ~50% relative IBTR reduction; current data suggests modern systemic therapy may attenuate boost necessity for lower-risk pts

⚠️ Retrospective registry design; no randomization; adequacy of systemic therapy not uniformly verified; 'uncertain' risk category poorly defined; absolute N in high-risk subgroups small (149-149 no-boost ≥3RF pts)
❓ Which specific pts within ≥3 RF group benefit from boost? Prospective de-escalation trial needed to validate omission strategy

verdict.rationale: Retrospective registry, no randomization; small N in high-risk subgroups; adequacy of systemic therapy unverified. Hypothesis-generating, not practice-changing.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, BCT, with or without boost, treated 2012-2016 in the Netherlands

studies.name: DBCG HYPO
studies.tldr: DBCG HYPO at 10yr: 40Gy/15fr reduces grade 2-3 breast induration (HR 0.76, p=0.005) vs 50Gy/25fr; no difference in recurrence or survival.
details: 📐 10-yr OS: 93.0% (40Gy) vs 92.1% (50Gy), HR 0.81 (0.63-1.04), p=0.10; no significant difference in LRR or distant failure
🔍 Phase III non-inferiority RCT, 1,882 node-negative breast/DCIS pts, 2009-2014 (Denmark/Norway/Germany); median f/u 12.8 yrs; morbidity assessed at yrs 0,1,2,3,4,5,10
🔗 Consistent with START-A/B (2008/2013) and FAST-Forward (2020) at longer follow-up; adds 10-yr morbidity durability to the hypofractionation evidence base
⚠️ Primary EP was 3-yr grade ≥2 induration (morbidity), not oncologic; recurrence and survival are prespecified secondary analyses
❓ Trial restricted to node-negative pts; applicability to node-positive pts receiving regional nodal RT or boost not addressed

verdict.rationale: Hypofractionation already standard post-START/FAST-Forward; 10-yr DBCG data adds morbidity durability consistent with prior randomised evidence.
verdict.audience: Node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS, adjuvant whole-breast RT

studies.name: IMPORT HIGH
studies.tldr: IMPORT HIGH 10yr: IBTR 3.5% (40+16Gy) vs 3.7% (48Gy SIB); 53Gy higher at 5.5%; SIB reaffirmed as SOC.
details: 🔍 Phase III 1:1:1 RCT, N=2617, 76 UK hospitals 2009-2015; pT1-3 pN0-3a M0 post-BCS requiring boost; annual f/u to 10yr
📊 OS abs diff vs 40+16Gy at 10yr: 48Gy -0.5% (95% CI -3.0, 2.8); 53Gy +1.5% (-1.4, 5.1) -- no OS signal either arm

details.text: 💊 Late AEs at 10yr (moderate/marked): low and similar across all arms

details: ⚠️ 53Gy arm IBTR numerically higher (5.5% vs 3.5-3.7%); no OS benefit; further dose escalation beyond 48Gy not supported
🔗 5yr data (Lancet 2023): 40+16Gy 1.9%, 48Gy 2.0%, 53Gy 3.2%; 10yr confirms -- all arms remain below original 5% control estimate
❓ Whether subgroups (high-grade, LVI+) within this higher-risk cohort might benefit from escalation beyond 48Gy not explored

verdict.rationale: 10yr follow-up of phase III; 5yr data (Lancet 2023) already established 48Gy SIB. Results consistent; no practice change at 10yr.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer pT1-3 pN0-3a M0, post-BCS, tumour bed boost required

studies.name: RAPCHEM
studies.tldr: RAPCHEM 10-year data: nodal-response-guided locoregional RT de-escalation yields <3% recurrence rate post-NAT.
details: 📊 10-year locoregional recurrence <3% with response-adapted RT de-escalation after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
🔍 nodal response assessed post-NAT; locoregional RT field de-escalated based on response, not omitted entirely
⚠️ complete RT omission not yet validated; ongoing prospective trials + NSABP B-51 required before adoption
🔗 B-51 (randomised, post-NAT ypN0 pts) is the key pending dataset for omission vs de-escalation question
❓ whether nodal pCR alone is sufficient to omit locoregional RT entirely remains open pending B-51 + prospective data

verdict.rationale: 10-year single-arm maturation of a de-escalation approach; no randomised comparator. Complete RT omission arm not yet validated.
verdict.audience: Early/locally advanced breast, post-NAT, nodal response-assessed

studies.name: APBI-IMRT Florence
studies.tldr: APBI-IMRT Florence: 15-yr IBTR 7.7% vs 4.2% (HR 1.57, p=0.17); no difference in locoregional control, DM, OS.
details: 🔍 Phase III RCT, N=520, 1:1 APBI (30Gy/5#) vs WBI (50Gy/25# + 10Gy boost); BCS pts, pT<25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40; enrolled 2005-2013
📐 HR 1.57 (95% CI 0.82-3.04), p=0.17 for IBTR; equivalence margin was delta 5%, 80% power
⚠️ IBTR excess in APBI arm driven by new ipsilateral primaries (5.9% vs 2.7%), not true local recurrence (2.1% vs 1.6%); biologically distinct events collapsed into same endpoint
⚠️ Equivalence design powered for 5-yr IBTR 3%; 15-yr follow-up is exploratory, not the pre-specified analysis window. Trial not powered to detect the observed numeric differences.

🔗 vs FAST-Forward and other APBI trials: longer follow-up here than most; IBTR signal at 15yr consistent with Florence 5yr (EJC 2015) and 10yr (JCO 2020) prior publications showing trend without significance
❓ Whether new ipsilateral primaries after APBI represent inadequate field coverage or independent biology remains unresolved; impacts how IBTR endpoint should be weighted in APBI decisions

verdict.rationale: Phase III with 15yr f/u; IBTR HR 1.57 not significant (p=0.17); excess driven by new primaries not local recurrence. No OS/DM difference. Supports APBI as viable option in selected pts.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, BCS, pT<25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40

studies.name: DBCG RT Natural
studies.tldr: DBCG RT Natural: PBI cut 4-yr LR to 1.5% vs 9.8% in low-risk BCS ≥60; RT+ET: 0 events; surgery alone insufficient.
details: 🔍 Phase III RCT, N=926; BCS ≥60yr pT1N0 grade 1-2 ER≥10%; PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI; ET per guideline; stopped at interim, median FU 4yr
📊 4-yr LR by RT × ET subgroup — comparison values omitted (cell number "1.3" not verified in source)
🔗 Discussant: RT or ET appear similar for low-risk subgroup; per EUROPA context RT favors QoL over ET in elderly BCS
⚠️ 5-yr primary EP not yet mature (4yr data); non-randomized self-selecting no-RT arm (n=278) adds observational bias
❓ ET substitution for RT in pT1a-b grade 1 pts unresolved; no OS or biomarker subgroup data in source

verdict.rationale: Phase III interim (4yr, 5-yr EP not mature); 9.8% LR without RT diverges from PRIME II / CALGB 9343 premise that RT omission is safe in elderly BCS.
verdict.audience: BCS ≥60yr, pT1N0 grade 1-2, ER≥10% HER2-normal, unifocal

open_questions: For 0-2 RF pts in the modern systemic era, does tumour-bed boost add measurable IBTR reduction or primarily adds late morbidity?
Does APBI's new ipsilateral primary excess at 15yr (Florence: 5.9% vs 2.7%) reflect inadequate CTV coverage or biologically independent events, and how should this weight patient selection?
Can nodal pCR alone validate locoregional RT omission, or does RAPCHEM's <3% LRR rate depend on residual de-escalated field coverage?

studies.name: RCC SBRT 5-year local control
studies.tldr: 100% local control at 5 years for primary RCC treated with SBRT, per tweet report.
details: 📊 1° EP: 100% local control at 5 years (verbatim from tweet; no additional effect sizes reported in source)
🔍 treatment modality: SBRT for primary RCC; phase, N, fractionation schema, and eligibility criteria not reported in source tweet
⚠️ no underlying data shared in tweet: no N, no fractionation details, no competing risks reported; headline LC figure unverifiable from this source alone
🔗 consistent with prior prospective SBRT-RCC series (IROCK consortium, Siva et al. 2018 Eur Urol): ~97-100% LC at 2-3 yr; 5-yr extension adds meaningful durability signal if confirmed
❓ ablative efficacy vs metastatic progression and OS not addressed; role vs partial nephrectomy or thermal ablation in operable pts remains open

verdict.rationale: Single tweet with no N, no phase, no fractionation; LC figure unverifiable. Directionally consistent with prior IROCK data but insufficient to change practice.
verdict.audience: Primary RCC (likely inoperable/oligometastatic), treated with SBRT

open_questions: Does 100% 5-yr LC with SBRT for primary RCC translate to OS equivalence vs partial nephrectomy in operable T1b disease, or is local control a surrogate obscuring competing oncologic risks?

studies.name: EXTEND
studies.tldr: EXTEND phase II RCT of MDT + SOC vs SOC alone in oligometastatic solid tumors: primary basket analysis published JCO May 2026.
details: 🔍 Phase II randomized basket trial; MDT (SBRT/SABR) added to SOC vs SOC alone; multiple solid tumor histologies enrolled across baskets
📊 No effect sizes reported in source tweets; primary aggregated analysis just published JCO (DOI 10.1200/JCO-25-02856); numbers not extractable from source content
🔗 ctDNA correlatives presented synchronously at ESTRO 2026; may define biological mechanism and selection signal for MDT benefit across histologies
⚠️ Basket design aggregates heterogeneous histologies; pooled analysis may obscure histology-specific HRs; individual basket estimates needed for clinical gating
❓ Which histology baskets drove the aggregated signal? Can ctDNA dynamics predict MDT benefit at the individual patient level?

verdict.rationale: Phase II randomized basket trial; no effect sizes in source. Histology heterogeneity and phase II maturity preclude SOC-level conclusions.

studies.name: OligoCARE
studies.tldr: OligoCARE: SBRT for OMD, 88.6% LC at 3yrs in 2447 pts; CRC lowest (19.6% LF), prostate best (8.1% LF).
details: 📊 Overall local in-field progression 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yrs (88.6% LC at 3yrs), median f/u 31mo
🔍 EORTC prospective registry Jul 2019-Jul 2025, 57 institutions, 2447 pts, 3533 lesions, median age 69, 69% male
🔍 Minimum PTV dose most critical technical factor for LC; de novo OMD outperformed repeat OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose
⚠️ No randomized comparator arm; OS/PFS not reported; interim registry analysis; histology mix limits direct cross-site generalization
🔗 Confirms real-world SBRT LC consistent with SABR-COMET (Lancet 2019) and STOMP/ORIOLE RCT local control signals across a far larger, multi-institutional cohort
❓ CRC radioresistance mechanism and optimal dose escalation/combination strategy unresolved; whether improved LC translates to OS benefit not addressed here

verdict.rationale: Large prospective multi-center registry; no randomized comparator, but N=2447 and 31mo f/u confirm real-world SBRT LC consistent with existing RCTs.

open_questions: Which histology baskets drove EXTEND's aggregated MDT signal, and can ctDNA dynamics prospectively identify individual pts who benefit?
OligoCARE's CRC 3yr local failure of 19.6% vs prostate 8.1% demands resolution: radioresistance mechanism or dose-adequacy gap?

studies.name: DIREKHT
studies.tldr: DIREKHT: post-op RT de-escalation in HNSCC via contralateral neck sparing and/or primary CTV dose reduction to 56 Gy.
details: 🔍 Post-op RT de-escalation trial in HNSCC; interventions include contralateral neck sparing and/or reduced primary CTV dose to 56 Gy (vs standard 60-66 Gy)
⚠️ No effect sizes, endpoints, or outcomes reported in source tweet; tweet references 'the details' via a truncated link with no numeric data
❓ Key open questions not addressable from source: which pts qualify for contralateral sparing, LRC/OS outcomes vs standard fractionation, toxicity reduction quantified

verdict.rationale: Source tweet contains no outcomes, endpoints, or effect sizes; trial design described only at conceptual level. Cannot classify without data.
verdict.audience: HNSCC, post-operative RT setting, selected pts eligible for contralateral neck sparing

bookmarks.text:
There are tremendous opportunities to improve post-operative radiotherapy in HNSCC. The DIREKHT trial is an excellent example of such work, in which they spared the contralateral neck in a specified group of patients and/or reduced the primary CTV dose to 56 Gy.  

The details… https://t.co/7W84LYIofR

0000g
Background
Metastasis-free survival (MFS) has been validated as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in non-metastatic prostate
cancer, and may represent an adaptable and clinically meaningful endpoint in oligorecurrent disease.
The phase II RADIOSA trial compared stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) alone (Arm A) versus SBRT plus 6-month
androgen deprivation therapy (ADT, Arm B). This post-hoe analysis evaluated MFS and eugonadal MFS after
testosterone recovery, with a visual mapping of disease trajectories through a Sankey diagram.
Methods
A total of 102 patients were randomized 1:l to Arm A (SBRT) or Arm B (SBRT + ADT).
Median follow-up (Reverse KM) was 49.23 months (95% C1 42.47-54.8).
MFS was defined as the time between randomisation and the appearance of a metastatic recurrence (any MI) on imaging.
Eugonadal MFS was calculated from testosterone recovery to new metastasis or last follow-up.
Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival curves were compared with log-rank tests; hazard ratios were estimated using Cox models.

Study aim
Given that a boost reduces the risk of IBTR by approximately 50%' and guided by the Assisi
thresholds?, the objective of our study is:
To determine 5-and 10-year IBTR rates in patients treated with or without a radiotherapy boost

Conclusions
• Low IBTR rates across all patient subgroups
• Based on the presence of combined risk factors, 5-and 10-year IBTR rates exceeded
the Assisi thresholds only in:
• Patients with ≥ 3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10 years
Take home messages
• The low IBTR rates across all subgroups suggest that
• In patients with 0-2 risk factors a tumour bed boost can be omitted, in the modern
systemic therapy era
• In patients with ≥ 3 risk factors the clinical relevance of a tumour bed boost remains
open to discussion
• Further research is needed to identify specific subgroups of patients who benefit from a
boost

Methods

Background & Methods
Background
Moderately hypofractionated adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy for early
node-negative breast cancer is standard worldwide, but long-term
randomised evidence remains limited. Here, we present the Danish
Breast Cancer Group HYPO trial prespecified 10-year outcomes.
Methods
• Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial (1:1) across Denmark,
Norway and Germany (2009-2014)
• 1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
• Whole-breast RT: 50 Gy/25 fractions vs 40 Gy/15 fractions
• Primary endpoint: 3-year grade 22 breast induration at two consequtive
visits, or at final follow-up
• Morbidity evaluated year 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10
• These results are the prespecified 10-year analyses of toxicity.

10-year Follow-Up of the BCG HYPO Trial: Breast
Induration, Recurrence and Survival After
Hypofractionated Whole Breast Irradiation
Conclusion & Take-Home Message
40 Gy / 15 fractions reduced long-term breast
induration without compromising recurrence or
survival outcomes.
At 12.8 years follow-up, results showed:
• Lower fibrosis risk with maintained cancer outcomes
• Supports continued use of moderately hypofractionated
whole-breast radiotherapy
Less toxicity. Same cancer
-control.

0000g
• At 10 years, IBTR event incidence is low in this higher-than-average risk breast cancer
group, whether boost is delivered sequentially or simultaneously
• Incidence in both 40+16Gy and 48Gy groups at 10 years remains lower than the 5%
estimate for control group at 5 years in original sample size calculations
• SIB is a safe treatment with reduced patient visits
• Further escalation of boost dose does not appear advantageous
• These results reaffirm use of 48Gy SIB in this population as standard of care

Discussion
• OLIGOMA is the first RCT to demonstrate a PFS-benefit from MDT in
patients with oligometastatic breast cancer.
• Majority had ER/PR pos. HER2 neg. disease in the first line-setting
• >80% of patients had 1-3 metastases, 2/3 of lesions were bone metastases.
• Short-term quality of life was not impaired by MDT.
• Recruitment was stopped early (< 20% of initial, < 40% of amended #)
• Numerous trials are still ongoing (incl. TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA,
CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI).
• Comprehensive analyses are needed to determine which patients benefit
most.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
"ESTRO
2026
At 15 years median of follow up, the phase III APBI-IMRT Florence trial (NCT02104895)
demonstrates durable oncological outcomes with PB in selected patients with early breast cancer
Although a numerical increase in IBTR was observed, this was largely attributable to new
primary tumours rather than true local recurrences.
No differences were observed in loco-regional control, distant metastasis, breast cancer-
specific survival or overall survival.
These findings provide mature evidence supporting PBl as a safe de-escalation strategy and
reinforce its role as a standard treatment option in appropriately selected patients

7. CONCLUSION
The PRIME trial is the first randomized phase Ill study evaluating 5-fraction SBRT with whole-pelvis RT and ADT in high-risk and/or
node-positive prostate cancer. Interim results from ESTRO 2026 demonstrate excellent safety, low toxicity, and comparable early
efficacy and QoL between extreme hypofractionation and moderate hypofractionation. These findings support SBRT (5 fractions) as
a convenient, effective, and well-tolerated option, pending mature 4-5 year outcomes.

SUMMARY
• POP-RT (2 years ADT) demonstrated significant benefit in bFFS, cFFS, and MFS with WPRT.
• PEACE-2 (3 years ADT, modern imaging, dose-escalated RT) showed no significant improvement in bPFS, MFS, CSS, or OS.
